candidate_problems.text: What exact boundary conditions make the conjecture false? The argument relies on 'finite minds' being unable to internalize all knowledge, exclude all benefits, or cheaply dominate others, but it leaves open a sharp characterization of when coercion, hierarchy, conquest, or monopolization can outperform cooperative equilibria for long periods. A key open problem is to identify the parameters—power asymmetry, surveillance, replication cost, cognitive substitutability, resource abundance/scarcity, and speed of adaptation—under which shared reality-maintenance remains indispensable versus when one actor can effectively privatize it.

How can agents distinguish genuine preference conflict from public-goods provision failure in practice? The conjecture turns on this distinction, but real systems mix both: some destructive behavior comes from malice or incompatible values, some from mistrust, and some from coordination failure. An important unresolved question is what observable signatures, institutional tests, or dynamic models can separate these cases well enough to guide intervention, since the remedies differ radically (deterrence, bargaining, subsidy, verification, constitutional design, etc.).

What mechanisms reliably solve the second-order public-goods problem of maintaining the cooperation-supporting layer itself? The conjecture says peace, truth-seeking norms, dispute resolution, and toleration are public goods, but then their production also requires enforcement, legitimacy, funding, and error-correction. The open problem is to explain which institutional architectures can stably produce these meta-public-goods without collapsing into free-riding, capture, censorship, or brittle centralization, especially under high uncertainty and strategic deception.
